content:
Tree-shaped decision rules predate modern ML — medical **flowcharts** and expert systems in the **1960s–70s** used hand-crafted if-then structures.

**Morgan & Sonquist** (1963) applied automated tree induction in social science (**AID**, Automatic Interaction Detection), splitting groups to explain variance in survey outcomes.

**CHAID** (Kass, 1980) added chi-square-based categorical splits. **ID3** (**Quinlan**, 1986) introduced **entropy** and **information gain** for discrete classification — the foundation of impurity-based splitting taught today.

**C4.5** (Quinlan, 1993) extended ID3 to continuous features, missing values, and pruning. In parallel, **CART** (**Breiman, Friedman, Olshen, Stone**, 1984) unified **classification and regression** trees with **Gini** or **entropy** for classes and **variance reduction** for regression — binary trees, cost-complexity pruning, and the algorithm behind most modern libraries.

**Breiman** later co-developed **Random Forests** (2001), combining many CART trees to fix single-tree instability. **Gradient boosting** (Friedman, 1999–2001) sequentially added trees to correct residuals — XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost scaled the idea to industry default for tabular data.

Today scikit-learn's `DecisionTreeClassifier` implements CART with Gini default; the same split mechanics propagate through every major tree ensemble.

timeline.label: AID (1963)
timeline.description: Early automated tree splitting for explaining variance in survey and social-science data.

timeline.label: ID3 (1986)
timeline.description: Quinlan introduces entropy-based information gain for building classification trees on discrete features.

timeline.label: CART (1984)
timeline.description: Breiman et al. publish the CART framework — binary splits, Gini/entropy, regression variance, and cost-complexity pruning.

timeline.label: C4.5 (1993)
timeline.description: Quinlan extends trees to continuous attributes, missing values, and rule pruning — influential in data mining.

timeline.label: Random Forests & boosting (1999–2001)
timeline.description: Ensembles of CART trees — bagging and boosting — address single-tree variance while retaining split-based learning.
